senses.definition: a pit or cavity into which something is blown or blown out, especially in a welding or metalworking context
examples: The metal was heated and the blowpit was used to guide the flame.
Workers adjusted the blowpit to control the airflow.

senses.definition: the point at which a blast or puff occurs; a term found in specialized technical contexts
examples: Set the blowpoint to release the gas safely.
The technician adjusted the blowpoint for optimal pressure.

senses.definition: able to resist being blown out or extinguished; resistant to damage from wind or explosion
examples: The blowproof shield withstood the blast.
They needed blowproof materials for the protective suit.

senses.definition: an act of striking or hitting something or someone; a gust of wind; a punch
examples: He took a hard blow to the temple.
A cold blow hit the sails.

senses.definition: a sudden bad news or misfortune; an unlucky event
examples: The project took a blow when funding was cut.
That setback dealt a blow to their plans.

senses.definition: to strike something or someone hard; to move with a sudden force
examples: The hammer blows the nail into place.
Wind blows hard from the north.

senses.definition: a south or informal term not widely standardized; may appear as a misspelling or dialectal form
examples: In some dialects, 'blowse' might appear as a variant of 'blows'.

senses.definition: past tense of blowse or a dialectal or erroneous form; not standard in contemporary English
examples: He blowsed the dust from the instrument.

senses.definition: plural form of a nonstandard or rare term; not widely used in standard English
examples: A collection of blowses appeared in the manuscript.

senses.definition: comparative form of 'blowsy'; messy or untidy in a humorous or informal way
examples: The workshop looked blowsier after the storm.
His desk is even blowsier than yesterday.

senses.definition: superlative form of 'blowsy'; most messy or untidy
examples: The room was the blowsiest of all the offices.

senses.definition: in a blowsy manner; in a messy or disordered way
examples: The papers lay blowsily across the desk.

senses.definition: the quality of being blowsy; disordered or untidy appearance
examples: The blowsiness of the room was obvious after the party.

senses.definition: plural of blowsiness; instances of disordered appearance
examples: There were several blowsinesses in the workshop.

senses.definition: a spray or aerosol used to produce a strong or fragrant effect, often for cosmetic or cleaning purposes
examples: She used a blowspray to refresh her outfit.

senses.definition: slang/colloquial for messy or disordered; provocative in British English
examples: Her apartment looks blowsy after the party.
That outfit is a bit blowsy for the meeting.

senses.definition: a sore or blister; archaic or rare usage; not common in modern standard English
examples: A blowth appeared on his skin after the burn.

senses.definition: a tool that uses a flame to burn or heat substances, often for welding or soldering
examples: He used a blowtorch to weld the metal pieces.

senses.definition: past tense of blowtorch in a figurative or literal sense; heated or burned with a blowtorch
examples: The metal was blowtorched to remove rust.

senses.definition: plural of blowtorch
examples: They bought several blowtorches for the workshop.

senses.definition: present participle of blowtorch; to apply heat with a blowtorch
examples: They were blowtorching the joints to seal them.

senses.definition: a tube used to blow air or gas, or a tube used in a blowing mechanism
examples: The compressor connects to the blowtube.

senses.definition: plural of blowtube
examples: The lab uses several blowtubes during experiments.

senses.definition: an explosion or a photograph enlargement
examples: The blowup scared the bystanders.
Make a blowup of the photo to see details.

senses.definition: to explode or to become very angry; to enlarge a photo
examples: The balloon will blow up if you overinflate it.
He blew up at the news.

senses.definition: plural of blowup; explosions or enlarged photographs
examples: There were several blowups during the demonstration.
They printed multiple blowups of the chart.

senses.definition: informal British spelling meaning windy or prone to blowing strongly
examples: A blowy day kept us indoors.
The winds were very blowy last night.

senses.definition: a nonstandard or rare term; may be a misspelling of 'blowzy' or 'blowz' in dialects
examples: Dialect speakers used 'blowze' to describe mess.
